op.name: broadcast_arrays
op.notes: Broadcast arrays against each other. Cost: numel(output).
op.summary: Broadcast any number of arrays against each other.

blocks.text: Broadcast any number of arrays against each other.

desc_blocks.text:
If True, then sub-classes will be passed-through, otherwise
the returned arrays will be forced to be a base-class array (default).

These arrays are views on the original arrays.  They are typically
not contiguous.  Furthermore, more than one element of a
broadcasted array may refer to a single memory location. If you need
to write to the arrays, make copies first. While you can set the
``writable`` flag True, writing to a single output value may end up
changing more than one location in the output array.

desc_blocks.name: deprecated:: 1.17
desc_blocks.body:
The output is currently marked so that if written to, a deprecation
    warning will be emitted. A future version will set the
    ``writable`` flag False so writing to it will raise an error.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. deprecated:: 1.17
    The output is currently marked so that if written to, a deprecation
    warning will be emitted. A future version will set the
    ``writable`` flag False so writing to it will raise an error.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> x = np.array([[1,2,3]])
>>> y = np.array([[4],[5]])
>>> np.broadcast_arrays(x, y)
(array([[1, 2, 3],
        [1, 2, 3]]),
 array([[4, 4, 4],
        [5, 5, 5]]))

blocks.text:
Here is a useful idiom for getting contiguous copies instead of
non-contiguous views.

blocks.code:
>>> [np.array(a) for a in np.broadcast_arrays(x, y)]
[array([[1, 2, 3],
        [1, 2, 3]]),
 array([[4, 4, 4],
        [5, 5, 5]])]
